title: A ZERO-WASTE LIFESTYLE
subtitle: For questions 1–8, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).
passage:
I am extremely concerned (0) ABOUT the amount of waste we produce. There is much [[1]] much of it. It uses up natural resources, takes up landfill space and, in the case of plastic, makes [[2]] way into our oceans. So, I follow the three Rs – reduce, reuse, recycle. I'm trying initially to generate as little rubbish [[3]] possible, but my ultimate aim is to lead a zero-waste lifestyle.

Recycling is important, but I do [[4]] best to avoid disposable items in favour of reusable ones. So, for example, [[5]] of paper towels, serviettes and tissues, I only ever use cloth ones. I always take several bags with me when I go shopping, and won't buy anything that's wrapped or comes in a container [[6]] the wrapping or container can be reused. [[7]] I realise what I do won't make a huge difference on its own, it might at least help to influence others. And if we all [[8]] our part, it would undoubtedly have a positive effect on the environment.

explanations.1: Much too much — emphasis with ‘too’ before another quantifying ‘much’.
explanations.2: Makes its way — fixed phrase; ‘its’ (not ‘it’s’) as possessive before ‘way’.
explanations.3: As … as possible — comparison with ‘little rubbish’ before ‘possible’.
explanations.4: Do my best — possessive determiner before ‘best’.
explanations.5: Instead of — contrast disposable paper with reusable cloth.
explanations.6: Unless — condition: only buy if the wrapping cannot be reused (unless it can be).
explanations.7: Although / Though / While — concession: acknowledges limits but stresses influence.
explanations.8: Do our part or play our part — fixed expressions meaning contribute one’s share.
